envelopes.hydrated_text:
Pitch design — the engineering of new pitches with intentional shapes — has become a routine part of player development. We analyse the public Statcast record from 2020 through 2025 and identify roughly 280 pitchers who introduced a substantively new pitch (defined as a 4+ mph velocity shift, a 6+ inch movement shift, or a new pitch type by Statcast classification) that survived as a meaningful workload share in the following season.

The new pitches are disproportionately sweepers (introduced by 92 pitchers), splitters (61), and sinkers redesigned for arm-side run (47). Cutters, despite recent popularity in player-development literature, were less commonly introduced (28) — possibly because cutter design requires unlearning fastball spin patterns that successful pitchers have refined over years.

The success rate, defined as a positive run-value contribution from the new pitch in its first full season, was 58%. Sweepers had the highest success rate (71%) and cutters the lowest (39%). We discuss the methodological challenges of evaluating pitch design causally, including the obvious survivorship bias in our sample.

Pitcher injury rates have continued their multi-decade rise, with 2025 setting a record for elbow surgeries in the majors and minors combined. We synthesise the available evidence on causal contributors and find that no single mechanism explains the trend; rather, several mutually reinforcing factors are implicated.

Velocity has continued to climb (average four-seam fastball 94.7 mph in 2025 vs 92.1 mph in 2015), which alone is associated with higher elbow stress in cadaver studies. Spin rates have also climbed, and high-spin pitch types tend to require finger-strain mechanics that several biomechanists have flagged. Workload patterns have shifted from many low-effort pitches to fewer high-effort pitches, removing the 'active recovery' benefit historically provided by low-leverage innings.

The most concerning recent finding is that injury rates among the 16-21 age group entering pro ball have risen even faster than major-league rates, suggesting the problem is upstream of professional player development. We discuss several proposed interventions, none of which has clear empirical support yet.

Three full seasons after the introduction of the pitch clock, we revisit the question of whether the rule change has had measurable downstream effects beyond game pace. The headline result on pace held: average game time stabilised at 2:38 in 2025, down from a pre-rule average of 3:11.

The more interesting findings are second-order. Stolen-base attempts have continued to climb beyond the immediate post-rule jump, settling at 0.84 attempts per game versus 0.51 pre-rule. Walk rate has climbed slightly, possibly because pitchers facing the clock have reduced ability to fully reset between high-stress pitches. Strikeout rate has dropped marginally, an unexpected result we attribute to reduced pitcher recovery time degrading high-effort pitch quality.

We see no clear injury-rate signal attributable to the pitch clock, contradicting some pre-introduction concerns, though the time series is short and confounded with other simultaneous trends.

bullets: Pitch design has produced ~280 substantively new pitches in 2020-2025, dominated by sweepers (71% success rate) and splitters; cutters are less common and less successful (39%) likely because they require unlearning established fastball spin patterns. (env-10-a)

Pitcher elbow injury rates continue to climb with no single causal explanation; velocity, spin-driven finger-strain mechanics, and workload pattern shifts are mutually reinforcing, and the trend is now visible in 16-21 age groups entering pro ball. (env-10-b)

Three seasons after the pitch clock, second-order effects include continued stolen-base climb (0.84 attempts/game), slight walk-rate rise, and unexpected strikeout-rate drop, with no clear pitch-clock-attributable injury signal. (env-10-c)

notes: boundary case: span 3.6h sits inside 4h window; cosine 0.74 well above 0.70 floor; 2 distinct hostnames (538 + theathletic) exactly meets MIN_DOMAINS=2; topical thread is MLB pitching
